summary: Jeff Atwood responds to criticism calling his blog 'the most dangerous programming blog' by demonstrating the value of critical reading. He takes a potentially inaccurate blog post about Deflate vs GZip performance, tests it himself, and discovers the original claim was partially correct but hardware-dependent. Rather than being angry about misinformation, he argues that treating blog readers as capable of critical thinking makes them better programmers. The post is a defense of blogging culture and reader intelligence.
key_points: Blog posts shouldn't be taken as gospel truth but as 'germs of intriguing ideas' that prompt investigation
Testing claims yourself leads to deeper understanding, even when the original advice is inaccurate
Dismissing blog content as 'dangerous' implies readers are too dumb to think critically
The best content emerges from discussion in comments, not the posts themselves
Technical claims can be context-dependent - the GZip vs Deflate performance varied significantly across different hardware
money_quotes: When you say that the information presented on a blog is 'dangerous,' you're implying the audience is too dumb or inept to read critically.
I am a better programmer for having read that blog post. Even though, near as I can tell, it's offering inaccurate advice.
The best content always begins where the blog post ends.
Like any veteran internet user, I never take what I read on a blog – or any other site on the internet, for that matter – as fact. Rather, it's a germ of an intriguing idea, a call to action.
Maybe what I promote on this blog is dangerous: thinking for yourself.
My audience is far, far smarter than I will ever be.
I, for one, have too much respect for my audience to ever do that.
key_insight: The 'danger' of programming blogs isn't misinformation—it's the assumption that readers can't evaluate claims critically and learn from the process of verification.
